abstract.text: List some iPhone devices which can use OUDS, i.e. supposed to support at least iOS 15.
Helps to define some metrics like tab bar heights.
Some font family cannot be used as they are because their “marketing” names are unknown to the OS
This dictionnary defines the expected PostScript names of font families known in iOS.
Depending to the font family, several rules are applied, with for example removal of whitespaces
and adding of prefixes of suffixes after the font family name or the weight.
), defines which type of iPhone it is

Can be used for sizes comparisons.

abstract.text: True if sizes greater or equal than XL (110 %) are used, accessible sizes too
WCAG 2.1 requirement for AAA level needs to have textual component matching 7:1 contrast ratio
and non textual component matching 4.5:1 contrast ratio.
This is recommended for mobiles devices projects (because of screen sizes and luminosity issues).
WCAG 2.1 requirement for AA level needs to have textual component matching 4.5:1 contrast ratio
and non textual component matching 3:1 contrast ratio.
This is recommended for web projects.
Returns the luminance of the given color using the WCAG 2.1 algorithm.
Color defined as red, green, blue, alpha tuple
The computed contrast ratio between two given colors
from its hexadecimal string representation, in RGB or RGBA format.
Returns a flag saying if the devic emodel has the home button, i.e. Touch ID, i.e. no safe area
library so as to logs things in standard output or elsewhere.
Returns if the device is in landscape mode if the current OS is iOS / iPadOS.
Some font families have names which cannot be used as they are to load fonts.
For example the “Helvetica Neue” font family must be loaded using the PostScript name “HelveticaNeue”.
The name of the font to load depends to its PostScript name and the weight.
For example a “bold Helvetica Neue” exists in iOS as “HelveticaNeue-Bold”
If the computed contrast ratio between two given colors meets the 4.5:1 requirement

Such values have been picked from the Apple Font Book.

Contains a set of helpers and extensions shared for higher level targets.
Computes accessibility contrast ratio between two colors in hexadecimal strings.
If the hexadecimal strings are colors format in RGBA, will use their format in RGB (i.e. apply opacity, not truncate).
If the computed contrast ratio between two given colors meets the 7:1 requirement
Some countries set up new laws so as to force companies to have a better accessibility of their digital services.
This is for example the case for France with the
The WCAG specifications, for AA level, defines a contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for textual elements (i.e. texts), and 3:1 for non-textual elements (i.e. link’s chevron).
However it is higly recommended for mobile devices to reach AAA level because of the screen sizes and luminosity issues, i.e. 7:1 for textual
This utility helps to define whether a set of contrast ratio matches AA and AAA or not.
